summary:
This course has been designed to provide students with an introduction to the main branches of mass media as well as practical experience or preparing content for these media.
I. Print media and journalism (Books, newspapers, magazines, comics, pamphlets +
outdoor media such as billboards, advertising etc)
II. Broadcast Media and journalism (Radio and television)
III. Digital media and journalism: New Age Media (Internet, mobile phones,
and computers)
goals: To learn about the development of the main types of mass media
To learn and understand debates and issues relating to mass media (freedom of information, speech, media control etc)
To learn the skills needed to produce content for the four main types of media (print journalism, broadcast journalism, digital journalism and copywriting)

plan.text:
Introduction by course instructor: Objectives, requirements and assessment
PRINT JOURNALISM
Lecture - What is International Journalism and Media? The opening lecture will look the nature of and growth of this aspect of transnational communication over the past few decades, and also the various forms that international journalism can take.
Workshop: In the first workshop we will begin working on the newsletter that is the first assessed project

PRINT JOURNALISM
Lecture: The press: Reporting as through the print media, including both 'broadsheet' and 'tabloid' newspapers. This lecture will examine the newspaper industry, news story writing styles, layout and production
Workshop: In this workshop we will continue work on the newsletter that is the first assessed project

PRINT JOURNALISM
Lecture: Glossies: the global magazine industry, magazine writing styles and production. This lecture will look at all of the above, with reference to realia
Workshop: In this workshop we will continue work on the newsletter that is the first assessed project

PRINT JOURNALISM
Lecture: In this final lecture, the future of print journalism in the digital age will be discussed
Workshop: Students will complete the final draft of the newsletter they have been preparing

PHOTOJOURNALISM
Lecture: This introductory lecture will examine the nature of photojournalism, with reference to real examples and discuss the relative merits of this form of journalism against other forms
Workshop: Students will begin preparing a photojournal (for this a camera will be required)

PHOTOJOURNALISM
Lecture: This second lecture will examine the practical aspects of photojournalism, with reference to real examples.
Workshop: Students will continue preparing their photojournal (for this a camera will be required)

BROADCAST JOURNALISM
Lecture title: 'On the box'. This lecture will examine the nature of television broadcast journalism, with reference to real examples. We will examine the rise of television, its impact on global society, as well as broadcasting styles
Workshop: Students will begin preparing a recorded news report (or news feature) that will be the third assessed project.

BROADCAST JOURNALISM
Lecture title: 'On the box II'. This second lecture will examine in further detail aspects of television broadcast journalism, with reference to real examples.
Students will also be introduced to practical aspects of television broadcast journalism
Workshop: Students will continue preparing the recorded news report (or news feature) that will be the third assessed project.

BROADCAST JOURNALISM
Lecture title: 'Wireless'. This lecture will examine the nature of radio broadcast journalism, with reference to real examples. We will examine the rise and decline of radio, its impact on global society, as well as broadcasting styles
Students will also be introduced to practical aspects of radio broadcast journalism
Workshop: Students will continue preparing the recorded news report (or news feature) that will be the third assessed project.

BROADCAST JOURNALISM
Lecture title: 'Wireless'. This lecture will examine the nature of radio broadcast journalism, with reference to real examples. We will examine the rise and decline of radio, its impact on global society, as well as broadcasting styles
Students will also be introduced to practical aspects of radio broadcast journalism

MULTIMEDIA JOURNALISM
Lecture: 'The digital age'. The opening lecture will examine the rise, spread and impact of digital media on international journalism, with reference to realia.
Students will also be introduced to practical aspects of online journalism
Workshop: For their final assignment students will begin preparing a news feature or story suitable for

MULTIMEDIA JOURNALISM
Lecture: 'The digital age'. The opening lecture will examine the rise, spread and impact of digital media on international journalism, with reference to realia.
Workshop: Students will continue preparing the news feature or story suitable for publishing on the internet

MULTIMEDIA JOURNALISM
Lecture: 'Networking'. The third lecture will examine the rise, spread and impact of social networking on international journalism, with reference to realia.
Workshop: Students will continue preparing the news feature or story suitable for publishing on the internet

MULTIMEDIA JOURNALISM
Lecture: The final lecture will look at the future of international journalism and media
Workshop: Students will continue preparing the news feature or story suitable for publishing on the internet

textbooks:
Study materials (Course Reader and Workbook) will be supplied by the teacher at the beginning of the semester. These are avaiable in both print and digital format, and can be downloaded from the accompanying MOODLE site for this course.
Participating students will require dictionary and notebook. Students will also be expected to make use of resources such as newspapers and magazines in the university libraries (メディアの森 and OASIS)
prereq:
Participants with an interest in understanding how the global media operates, and the practicalities of producing print, broadcast and digital media journalism
Students should have a reasonable command of written and spoken English. For both lectures and for instruction, English is the medium.
prep:
Participants are expected to spend at least 60 mins each week outside of classtime
Reading the course materials
Completing the exercises in the Workbook
Researching and preparing the news stories that they are required to write
teachers: ◎ マイケル シャープ
